file_name: GSITECHNOLOGYINC_11_16_2009-EX-10.2-INTELLECTUAL PROPERTY AGREEMENT between SONY ELECTRONICS INC. and GSI TECHNOLOGY, INC..txt

perturbation.original_text: Should either Party fail to observe faithfully and materially perform each of the material obligations assumed by it in this IP Agreement for thirty (30) days after its attention has been directed to any such breach by notice in writing from the other Party, the non-breaching Party shall, at its option, have the right to terminate the license granted by it under this Agreement, such termination to be effected by serving notice in writing upon the breaching Party to that effect on or after the expiration of such period of thirty (30) days.
perturbation.changed_text: Should either Party fail to observe faithfully and materially perform each of the material obligations assumed by it in this IP Agreement for fifteen (15) days after its attention has been directed to any such breach by notice in writing from the other Party, the non-breaching Party shall, at its option, have the right to terminate the license granted by it under this Agreement, such termination to be effected by serving notice in writing upon the breaching Party to that effect on or after the expiration of such period of fifteen (15) days.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the cure period from 30 to 15 days may conflict with contract law principles requiring reasonable notice and opportunity to cure breaches, especially in California where the contract is governed.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code Section 1657 states that 'If no time is specified for the performance of an act required to be performed, a reasonable time is allowed.' Reducing the cure period to 15 days may be deemed unreasonable, depending on the complexity of the breach, potentially conflicting with this statute and making the termination clause unenforceable.

OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 2. CONTRACTS [1549 - 1701] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 3. INTERPRETATION OF CONTRACTS [1635 - 1663] ( Title 3 enacted 1872. ) 1657. If no time is specified for the performance of an act required to be performed, a reasonable time is allowed. If the act is in its nature capable of being done instantly—as, for example, if it consists in the payment of money only—it must be performed immediately upon the thing to be done being exactly ascertained. (Enacted 1872.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the cure period from 30 to 15 days, which could be deemed unreasonable under California Civil Code Section 1657. The Law Explanation states that 'If no time is specified for the performance of an act required to be performed, a reasonable time is allowed.' Scraped Law Snippet 1, which contains the exact text of California Civil Code Section 1657, supports this. The Perturbation Explanation further highlights that this reduction may conflict with contract law principles requiring reasonable notice and opportunity to cure breaches, especially in California where the contract is governed. Therefore, reducing the cure period introduces an inconsistency that contradicts the principle of 'reasonable time' established by law, creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: All notices and other communications required or permitted hereunder will be in writing and, unless otherwise provided in this IP Agreement, will be deemed to have been duly given when delivered in person or when dispatched by electronic facsimile transfer (confirmed in writing by mail simultaneously dispatched) or one business day after having been dispatched by a nationally recognized overnight courier service to the appropriate Party at the address specified below:
perturbation.changed_text: All notices and other communications required or permitted hereunder will be in writing and, unless otherwise provided in this IP Agreement, will be deemed to have been duly given when delivered by carrier pigeon, smoke signal, or telepathy.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing standard notification methods (mail, courier) with archaic or unreliable ones (carrier pigeon, telepathy) makes the notice provisions commercially unreasonable and unenforceable. This can create legal problems.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code § 1636 states that a contract must be so interpreted as to give effect to the mutual intention of the parties as it existed at the time of contracting, so far as the same is ascertainable and lawful. Using unreliable notification methods is neither the intention of the parties nor is it reasonable given the circumstances.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1636 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> Code Search >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 2. CONTRACTS [1549 - 1701] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 3. INTERPRETATION OF CONTRACTS [1635 - 1663] ( Title 3 enacted 1872. ) 1636. A contract must be so interpreted as to give effect to the mutual intention of the parties as it existed at the time of contracting, so far as the same is ascertainable and lawful. (Enacted 1872.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text replaces standard notification methods (mail, courier) with unreliable ones (carrier pigeon, smoke signal, telepathy). This contradicts California Civil Code § 1636, as stated in the Law Explanation, because it undermines the 'mutual intention of the parties' to have a reasonable and ascertainable method of communication. Scraped Law Snippet 1 directly quotes California Civil Code § 1636 which states that a contract must be interpreted to give effect to the 'mutual intention of the parties as it existed at the time of contracting, so far as the same is ascertainable and lawful.' The new methods are not commercially reasonable, and therefore undermine the enforceability of the contract. The change clearly contradicts the legal obligation.

perturbation.original_text: If any action at law or in equity is necessary to enforce or interpret the terms of this IP Agreement, the Person prevailing shall be entitled to recover in such action its reasonable attorneys' fees, costs and necessary disbursements in addition to any other relief to which it may be entitled.
perturbation.changed_text: If any action at law or in equity is necessary to enforce or interpret the terms of this IP Agreement, the Person prevailing shall not be entitled to recover in such action its attorneys' fees, costs and necessary disbursements in addition to any other relief to which it may be entitled.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the attorneys' fees provision could be problematic. California law gives judges discretion to award attorney fees in some cases even if a contract does not provide for them.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Code of Civil Procedure Section 1021 states that parties are generally responsible for their own attorney's fees unless otherwise provided by statute or agreement. While this change doesn't outright violate the statute, it removes a standard provision that ensures the prevailing party is compensated, which can affect the fairness and enforceability of the agreement.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CCP 1021. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> Code Search >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Code of Civil Procedure - CCP PART 2. OF CIVIL ACTIONS [307 - 1062.34] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 14. OF MISCELLANEOUS PROVISIONS [989 - 1062.34] ( Title 14 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 6. Of Costs [1021 - 1038] ( Chapter 6 enacted 1872. ) 1021. Except as attorney’s fees are specifically provided for by statute, the measure and mode of compensation of attorneys and counselors at law is left to the agreement, express or implied, of the parties; but parties to actions or proceedings are entitled to their costs, as hereinafter provided. (Amended by Stats. 1986, Ch. 377, Sec. 2.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts the standard practice implied by California Code of Civil Procedure Section 1021. The original text allows for the prevailing party to recover attorney's fees, while the changed text explicitly denies this recovery. While the law states that attorney's fees are subject to agreement, expressly removing a provision for such fees undermines the ability of the prevailing party to be compensated, creating a legal contradiction as it deviates from established legal norms and expectations for such agreements. The scraped law snippet states, "Except as attorney’s fees are specifically provided for by statute, the measure and mode of compensation of attorneys and counselors at law is left to the agreement, express or implied, of the parties," which implies that parties can agree to attorney's fees, but the changed contract actively prevents this. Therefore, the removal constitutes a legal contradiction.
